initial_claims.description: The contract uses a misnamed constructor in Solidity 0.4.15. Because the contract is named Rubixi but the constructor-like function is named DynamicPyramid, it is a normal public function that any address can call to become creator.
initial_claims.evidence: Contract name is `Rubixi`, but ownership is set in `function DynamicPyramid() { creator = msg.sender; }`. In Solidity <0.5, only a function matching the contract name is treated as constructor. Any attacker can call DynamicPyramid() after deployment, set `creator` to themselves, then invoke `changeOwner`, `changeMultiplier`, `changeFeePercentage`, `collectAllFees`, `collectFeesInEther`, or `collectPercentOfFees` as the owner.

claim.description: The contract uses a misnamed constructor in Solidity 0.4.15. Because the contract is named Rubixi but the constructor-like function is named DynamicPyramid, it is a normal public function that any address can call to become creator.
claim.evidence: Contract name is `Rubixi`, but ownership is set in `function DynamicPyramid() { creator = msg.sender; }`. In Solidity <0.5, only a function matching the contract name is treated as constructor. Any attacker can call DynamicPyramid() after deployment, set `creator` to themselves, then invoke `changeOwner`, `changeMultiplier`, `changeFeePercentage`, `collectAllFees`, `collectFeesInEther`, or `collectPercentOfFees` as the owner.

verdict.reasoning: The vulnerable pattern exists in the code. The contract is declared as `contract Rubixi`, but the ownership-setting function is `function DynamicPyramid() { creator = msg.sender; }`. In Solidity 0.4.15, a constructor must have the same name as the contract, so `DynamicPyramid()` is not a constructor here; it is a normal public function callable after deployment. There is no modifier or other restriction on `DynamicPyramid()`, so any external account can call it and overwrite `creator`. The `onlyowner` modifier is `if (msg.sender == creator) _;`, so once an attacker has called `DynamicPyramid()`, they satisfy the owner check and can call privileged functions including `collectAllFees()`, `collectFeesInEther(uint)`, `collectPercentOfFees(uint)`, `changeOwner(address)`, `changeMultiplier(uint)`, and `changeFeePercentage(uint)`. No code-backed mitigation blocks this path. The exploit is realistic: attacker sends a transaction calling `DynamicPyramid()`, becomes `creator`, then calls fee-withdrawal or admin functions. Impact is real and affects others: the attacker can steal accumulated fees and alter contract parameters, compromising control of the contract. The defender's argument is consistent with the code and correctly identifies the absence of any protection on this path.
verdict.recommendation: Rename the constructor to match the contract name for Solidity 0.4.15 (`function Rubixi() public { creator = msg.sender; }`) or, preferably, upgrade to a newer Solidity version and use the `constructor()` syntax. Also make ownership initialization one-time only and consider using `require(msg.sender == creator)` in the modifier for clarity.

The vulnerable pattern is present exactly as claimed. In Solidity 0.4.15, only a function whose name matches the contract name is treated as a constructor. The contract is named `Rubixi`, but the ownership-setting function is `DynamicPyramid()`, so it is an ordinary publicly callable function. There is no code-level mitigation restricting calls to `DynamicPyramid()`. Because `onlyowner` checks `msg.sender == creator`, any attacker can first call `DynamicPyramid()` to set `creator = msg.sender`, then successfully call all `onlyowner` functions.

There is no modifier or other restriction on `DynamicPyramid()`, so any external account can call it and overwrite `creator`. The `onlyowner` modifier is `if (msg.sender == creator) _;`, so once an attacker has called `DynamicPyramid()`, they satisfy the owner check and can call privileged functions including `collectAllFees()`, `collectFeesInEther(uint)`, `collectPercentOfFees(uint)`, `changeOwner(address)`, `changeMultiplier(uint)`, and `changeFeePercentage(uint)`. No code-backed mitigation blocks this path. The exploit is realistic: attacker sends a transaction calling `DynamicPyramid()`, becomes `creator`, then calls fee-withdrawal or admin functions.
